purpose: Pin the L0 approver canonicalization algorithm so Python (backend/services/constitution.py::_canonical_approver_key) and Rust (memra-core/src/governance/mod.rs::canonicalize_approver) cannot silently diverge. A divergence here is a real bypass: an attacker registering as 'alice' on one backend and 'Alice' on the other could pass the 3-approval gate twice. Both backends MUST agree byte-for-byte on every entry in `shared_cases`. The `known_divergences` block lists the two inputs where Rust `to_lowercase()` and Python `casefold()` legitimately differ (German ß, Greek final sigma); both backends MUST match their own expected value AND the two values MUST stay distinct. If a future change accidentally collapses a known_divergence into agreement, that's progress and the case should move to shared_cases.

shared_cases._note: U+E0100 in surrogate pair JSON encoding
U+E0061 in surrogate pair JSON encoding
Both Python casefold and Rust to_lowercase produce 'i + combining dot above' for U+0130 — confirmed by direct probe 2026-04-19
Out-of-scope for canonical dedup (mixed-script homoglyphs need actor_id); both backends fold the case but keep the Cyrillic codepoint distinct from Latin

known_divergences.rationale: Python casefold() expands ß → ss per Unicode case folding. Rust str::to_lowercase() does Unicode lowercase, which keeps ß. Closing this requires the `caseless` crate or hand-rolled folding on the Rust side — explicitly deferred.

Rust str::to_lowercase() applies the contextual Greek final-sigma rule (terminal Σ → ς). Python str.casefold() does not — every Σ becomes σ. Same root cause as the ß case (casefold ≠ to_lowercase semantics).
